*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined For each of five felony grades, the court’s choice of a maximum prison sentence yields a range of minimum terms the court may select in conjunction within that maximum. The minimum term can be no less than one year and no more than 40 percent of the judicial maximum sentence. Nevada has a multi-layered system of good time and earned time credits which are deducted from incarcerated people's minimum and maximum terms. The advancement of parole release eligibility is limited to 58 percent of the minimum term.
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility In 2020, there were 1,903 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 29 percent of people* in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 26 percent. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 37 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Sex In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 94 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 41 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
### Offense Types In 2020, 36 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 63 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for public order (34%) and property (22%) offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Sentence Lengths In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 60 percent of in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## State Notes "What if we change the section from Missing Data to more of a high-level state-specific Methods section? It can include: 1) Total N for the state 2) How we determined parole eligibility (e.g., what formulas did we use to estimate parole eligibility year) 3) Missing data patterns" - Jess S.


# Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 19 percent, changing from 12,653 in 2010 to 10,202 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2022, most people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 37 percent of people in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Sex In 2022, most people in prison were males, representing 92 percent of people in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most people in prison were between 25 to 34 years old, representing 31 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for public order, representing 29 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 29 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::


# Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 1 percent, dropping from 5,985 in 2010 to 5,938 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 51 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 32 percent increase compared to 2010. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, most people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 45 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Sex In 2020, most people released from prison were males, representing 85 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most releases from prison were people between 25 to 34 years old, representing 38 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, most people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses, representing 35 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
# Disparities ## Disparities in Incarceration ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity
::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ### Gender
:::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: Hispanic people spent 0.3 more years in prison compared to White people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, people of Other race(s) people spent 9.3 more years in prison on average compared to Black people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender On average, females spent 1 fewer years in prison compared to males in Nevada. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: For other violent offenses offenses, males spent 3.7 more years in prison on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::

## How Disparities Are Calculated While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. *States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.*

:::
